scientificName: Juglans spp. (J. nigra, J. regia), Castanea spp., Corylus spp., Juglans cinerea
description: Toronto supports several nut-bearing tree species including black walnut (Juglans nigra), English walnut (J. regia), butternut (J. cinerea), chestnuts (Castanea spp.), and hazelnuts (Corylus spp.). Black walnut is the most common, found as a large native shade tree throughout the city's parks, ravines, and residential streets. Butternut is a native species now threatened by butternut canker disease, while English walnuts are occasionally found in sheltered garden settings. Chestnuts and hazels are less common but increasingly planted in urban food forests and permaculture projects.

identification.leaves: Walnut and butternut leaves are large pinnately compound (30–60 cm long) with 11–23 leaflets, aromatic when crushed, turning yellow in autumn. Chestnut leaves are simple, oblong, 15–20 cm long with coarse, bristle-tipped teeth. Hazel leaves are rounded, 6–12 cm, with doubly-serrated margins and a slightly fuzzy texture.
identification.bark: Black walnut bark is dark brown-black with deep diamond-shaped furrows—highly distinctive on mature trees. Butternut bark is lighter grey with broad, flat-topped ridges. Chestnut bark develops spiralling fissures with age, while hazel has smooth, grey-brown bark with horizontal lenticels on its multi-stemmed form.
identification.flowers: Walnut trees produce long, pendulous male catkins (8–12 cm) in May alongside tiny reddish female flowers at branch tips. Hazels are remarkable for blooming in late winter (February–March), with bright yellow male catkins and tiny red female flowers visible on warm days before any leaves appear.
identification.fruit: Black walnuts are round (4–6 cm) with a thick green husk that stains black, enclosing an extremely hard, ridged shell. Chestnuts develop inside spiny burrs that split open in autumn to reveal 1–3 glossy brown nuts. Hazelnuts form in leafy husked clusters, each containing a smooth, round nut about 1.5–2 cm across.

ripening: Black walnuts drop from Toronto trees in late September through October—collect them promptly from the ground before squirrels cache them all. Chestnuts fall from their burrs in October and must be gathered quickly to avoid wormy nuts. Hazelnuts ripen in September to October, often harvested slightly early to beat squirrel competition. Butternuts ripen in September.

foraging.bestTime: Collect black walnuts from the ground in October after they naturally fall, choosing nuts with intact green-to-yellow husks. Chestnuts should be gathered daily in October as they fall from their burrs. Hazelnuts can be picked when husks begin to brown in September, then dried to finish ripening.
foraging.storage: Black walnuts must be husked immediately (wear gloves—the husk stains permanently), then cured in shells for 2–4 weeks before cracking. Shelled nuts freeze well for over a year. Chestnuts are perishable and should be refrigerated and used within 2–3 weeks, or frozen. Hazelnuts dry and store easily in-shell for months.
foraging.uses: Black walnuts have an intense, distinctive flavour excellent in baking, ice cream, and confections—unlike any commercial walnut. Chestnuts are unique among nuts for being starchy rather than oily, delicious roasted, puréed, or candied. Hazelnuts are rich and buttery, perfect for baking, homemade Nutella-style spreads, or eating raw. Butternut has a milder, butterier flavour than black walnut.
foraging.safety: All these nuts are safe to eat once properly prepared. Black walnut husks contain juglone, which stains skin brown-black and is toxic to some plants—always wear gloves when husking. People with tree nut allergies should obviously avoid all species. Verify butternut identity carefully as the endangered status means harvest should be limited.

ecologicalValue: Nut trees are keystone species in Toronto's urban ecosystem, providing high-energy food for squirrels, chipmunks, blue jays, and other wildlife that cache and disperse their seeds. Black walnut's juglone-rich leaf litter creates specialized habitat conditions, and the trees support numerous insect species including the luna moth. Butternut is listed as endangered in Canada due to butternut canker, making healthy urban specimens valuable for genetic conservation and recovery efforts.
